text: <par>Lahu <latinform>qhɛ̀ʔ</latinform> 'to be notched'; probably so called because of the notched appearance of the corona glandis.</par>

<par>The sibilant prefix might be a reduction of PTB <reconstruction>*sya</reconstruction> 'flesh; animal'. This prefix occurs productively with body parts in a number of other TB languages (e.g. Nung <latinform>sərö</latinform> 'bone', WT <latinform>skra</latinform> 'hair', Dimasa <latinform>salai</latinform> 'tongue', WT <latinform>snabs</latinform> 'snot', etc.).</par>

<par>The <latinform>lt-</latinform> cluster in the WT form looks like a metathesized version of the <latinform>tl-</latinform> cluster found in Kham, Bantawa, Lakher (Mara), and Miju (<xref ref="517">#517</xref>), further justifying the treatment of <reconstruction>*s-lay</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*s-tay</reconstruction> as co-allofams of the same etymon.</par>

<par>For now we are including this Abor-Miri form for 'self' with zero-initial in this set, along with several zero-initial forms meaning 'I/me' from Naga languages. One can imagine a scenario whereby the <reconstruction>*s-l</reconstruction> cluster became a voiceless lateral <reconstruction>*hl-</reconstruction>, thence a plain <reconstruction>*h-</reconstruction>, then zero. (Such was the fate, e.g. of the voiceless PLB resonants <reconstruction>*hl-</reconstruction>, <reconstruction>*hr-</reconstruction>, <reconstruction>*hy-</reconstruction>, which all became <latinform>h-</latinform> in Lahu. Cf. also the Tangkhul reflexes in <latinform>h-</latinform>.)</par>
<par>JAM: <latinform>tûr</latinform> = 'alive'? + 1321 or 2118? What is ~ ? (supposed to be OVER the e?)</par>

<par>It is possible that this Angami form is a borrowing from <xref ref="5636">#5636</xref>; however, the <latinform>ú-</latinform> prefix is evidently of TB origin, occurring in many body-part words.</par>
<par>Jäschke's definition (p. 142): "so, thus; in ancient literature regularly placed after words or thoughts that are literally quoted".</par>
<par>cf. Luquan Lolo <latinform>dʑe³³</latinform> LIVESTOCK (MXL-LQYi) (ref. < LTSR Lü-Ch’ üan).</par>
<par>This Lahu form is probably a loan from Burmese, since native Tone *2 syllables with voiceless fricative initials acquire Lahu very-low tone /<latinform> ̄</latinform>/. </par>

<par>Lahu <latinform>-chî</latinform> was previously identified (cf. DL:221) with the verb <latinform>chî</latinform> 'to lift up, exalt' since curries or vegetable dishes 'lift up' the taste of plain rice; most Lahu consultants endorse this analysis), but Akha has both <latinform>tjhì</latinform> [ILH] 'lift up' and <latinform>tshɛ̀</latinform> 'food (except rice)', which might indicate that two separate morphemes are involved (cf. <xref ref="152">#152</xref>).</par>
<par>This is really a Middle Chinese form.</par>
<par>“... staunch, immovable; safe, sure; definite, decided.” </par>

<par>The Lahu expression <latinform>cú phâʔ</latinform> means 'to wean', but the second syllable descends from PLB <reconstruction>*pak</reconstruction> 'take apart; dismantle; separate from' (LTSR #64), not from <reconstruction>*prat</reconstruction>. (The regular Lahu reflex of <reconstruction>*-at</reconstruction> is <latinform>-eʔ</latinform>.)</par>
<par>The second syllable of this form means 'change, exchange' < PTB <reconstruction>*s-lay</reconstruction> (see STC #283, HPTB p. 208, 216, 217).</par>
<par>The very low tone of the Lahu reflex points to a PLB Tone 2 etymon with *glottalized initial.</par>
